name: Project architecture overview
description: pi-agent-orchestrator is a Pi package/extension for explicit multi-agent orchestration. extensions/multi-agent/index.ts registers Pi tools, slash commands, and session lifecycle hooks; extensions/multi-agent/server.ts serves the React dashboard plus REST/SSE APIs; extensions/multi-agent/spawn.ts creates bwrap-isolated child Pi RPC agents in git worktrees; extensions/delegate-agent.ts is copied into sub-agent worktrees to provide sibling delegation. The dashboard is static React/TypeScript in web/ and is built by `bun run build`.

title: Orchestration is explicit and dashboard-backed, not TUI-invasive
rationale: The root Pi session only creates sub-agents after `/orchestrate` enables orchestration mode. The extension uses a headless HTTP server with REST and SSE for the browser dashboard and intentionally avoids modifying the Pi TUI, keeping the terminal responsive and clean.

title: Orchestrator Libraries are user-owned namespaced resource repos
rationale: Version-controlled agent types/templates should not be stored in the package directory. A configured Orchestrator Library is a user/team folder with a valid orchestrator-library.json manifest. The manifest name is a unique namespace; duplicate library names are rejected. Orchestrator resource refs use readable colon syntax such as `team-ai:skills/code-review`, while package-shipped resources remain read-only examples/fallbacks.

name: Orchestrator Library Phase 1 implementation map
description: Orchestrator Library backend foundation lives in extensions/multi-agent/orchestrator-library.ts. It parses/validates orchestrator-library.json, enforces unique manifest namespaces across configured libraries, reads/writes piAgentOrchestrator.libraries settings, discovers resources from manifest-defined directories, bootstraps starter libraries, and exposes discovery through /api/orchestrator-libraries plus dashboard read/onboarding UI in web/app.tsx.

name: Extension template smoke tests use temporary agents plus runtime tool snapshots
description: Dashboard validation for extension templates should spawn a throwaway Pi RPC agent with the resolved template extensions, wait briefly for the delegate extension's runtime-tools.json snapshot, report tool conflicts/diagnostics to the dashboard, then kill the process and remove its worktree without adding it to the live agents map.

title: Package examples are hidden via project display settings, not removal
rationale: The package pio-example resources remain shipped and visible by default for onboarding. Users can hide them per project with piAgentOrchestrator.showPackageExamples=false; dashboard APIs filter read-only package example agent types from listing only, while underlying discovery remains available for code paths that need it.

title: New agent/template create flows target Orchestrator Libraries when configured
rationale: Agent definitions and skill/extension templates are now discovered from configured Orchestrator Libraries and save to the first valid library's manifest-configured resource directory when a library exists. Legacy project .pi folders remain readable/fallback for existing resources and no-library setups, but configured libraries are the preferred write target.

title: Native Pi resource paths are hidden behind Orchestrator Libraries
rationale: The dashboard no longer exposes Skill & Extension Paths as a primary tab. Raw Pi skills/extensions settings remain available only as an advanced native escape hatch inside the Orchestrator Libraries panel, while extension discovery now includes Orchestrator Library extensions after native/npm sources so library-managed extension templates are the preferred path.

title: Root orchestrator profiles support skills/instructions, not arbitrary extensions
rationale: The interactive root Pi shell already runs the pi-agent-orchestrator extension, so root orchestration tools should come from the package itself. Orchestrator profiles may contribute root-only skills/prompts/instructions via Pi resource discovery/reload or system-prompt injection, but should not auto-load arbitrary Orchestrator Library extension files into the root shell. Users who need different root extension behavior should install trusted Pi extensions normally or fork/customize the orchestrator package.

title: Dashboard UI is being refactored into vertical feature modules
rationale: web/app.tsx had grown into a monolithic dashboard file, making it hard for agents to locate issue-relevant code. The agreed direction is incremental tracer-bullet extraction into domain-named modules under web/features while App remains the Dashboard Shell for tabs, SSE/live session state, and top-level wiring. Orchestrator Libraries owns the nested Native Pi Resource Settings UI even though native settings are a distinct domain concept.

name: Dashboard feature module map
description: Dashboard Shell remains in web/app.tsx for tabs, SSE/live session state, shared datasets, refresh wiring, and cross-feature dialog orchestration. Feature UI now lives in web/features/live-agents, web/features/agent-types, web/features/orchestrator-libraries, web/features/skill-library, and web/features/templates; shared dashboard types live in web/shared/dashboard-types.ts.

title: Prefer local-first Docker outer environment over per-agent bwrap for simple trusted orchestration
rationale: For local/trusted use, run the Pi orchestrator inside a Docker/devcontainer-style environment to get reproducible dependencies and host isolation, then spawn child agents as normal processes with git worktrees instead of maintaining per-agent bwrap flags. Worktrees remain the primary code isolation and issue handoff boundary; stronger per-agent sandboxing can remain a future pluggable runtime rather than the default path.

name: Root Orchestrator Profiles dashboard implementation map
description: Root Orchestrator Profile management now spans extensions/multi-agent/root-profiles.ts for discovery/detail/save/copy/delete with hash/read-only guards, extensions/multi-agent/server.ts for /api/root-profiles endpoints, web/features/root-orchestrator-profiles/RootOrchestratorProfilesPanel.tsx for list/preview/create/copy/edit/delete UI, and web/app.tsx for the Dashboard Shell Root Profiles tab and refresh wiring. Profiles save to the selected Orchestrator Library orchestratorProfiles manifest path when chosen, otherwise project .pi/orchestrator-profiles fallback.

Extension Template smoke tests reported zero active tools for real extensions even though `pi.getAllTools()` showed tools because the delegate runtime snapshot treated `pi.getActiveTools()` results as ToolInfo objects.
resolution: `pi.getActiveTools()` returns active tool names (`string[]`). Runtime reporting now enriches those names from `pi.getAllTools()`, the snapshot reader accepts legacy string-name active lists, and smoke tests wait for active startup snapshots plus run a minimal activation turn if startup still reports none.

title: Orchestration Protocol Mode will enforce packet-gated handoffs at operation boundaries
rationale: New-agent orientation should leverage the orchestrator hierarchy instead of requiring every agent to rediscover the repo. Protocol artifacts (Repo Atlas, Issue Context Packet, Area Dossier, Builder Packet, Completion Report) become the context product, with Pi tool_call/agent_end hooks and role-specific toolsets enforcing valid handoffs. Repo Atlas must be loaded lazily only when orchestration mode is enabled, and leads remain planners/synthesizers rather than orchestrators with broad spawn/send powers.

title: Protocol mode should enforce context-budget handoffs
rationale: Long-running spawned agents should operate below a healthy context range (target under ~60%, mandatory handoff around ~70%) because quality degrades as context saturates. When the budget is exceeded, the orchestrator should request a structured handoff report, update/store resumable state, retire the saturated agent, and spawn a fresh replacement with a compact continuation packet. Lead handoffs are a safety valve and diagnostic that the issue may be too broad or the orientation packet too weak.

name: Roadmap backend overview implementation map
description: Read-only Roadmap v1 backend lives in extensions/multi-agent/roadmap.ts, which parses .seeds/issues.jsonl into dashboard-facing RoadmapIssue/RoadmapOverview DTOs, computes ready/blocked/backlog/closed/in-progress groups and dependency maps, and is exposed through GET /api/roadmap in extensions/multi-agent/server.ts. Seeds naming is limited to source metadata so future storage can replace the provider behind the Roadmap API.

name: Roadmap dashboard feature module map
description: Roadmap dashboard UI starts in web/features/roadmap/RoadmapPanel.tsx and is wired as the Roadmap tab from web/app.tsx. The first tracer keeps the feature read-only, fetches GET /api/roadmap directly from the feature module, and renders summary counts plus small Next Up/Blocked previews; later hierarchy/detail/filter work should extend this module rather than moving Roadmap logic back into the dashboard shell.

name: Roadmap hierarchy UI implementation map
description: Roadmap tracer 3 added web/features/roadmap/roadmap-view-model.ts for pure epic grouping and dependency badge metadata, with UI rendering in web/features/roadmap/RoadmapPanel.tsx. Epics are top-level groups, direct dependency links attach active/closed children, ungrouped active work remains visible, and issue cards surface unresolved blockers plus dependent counts. Future detail/filter work should reuse the view model rather than recomputing hierarchy in components.

title: Roadmap hierarchy infers epic children from distinctive shared labels
rationale: Seeds dependency edges represent blocking semantics, not parent/child membership. Roadmap epics and tracer issues may not be dependency-linked, so the UI view model now groups unassigned issues under epics by distinctive shared labels (for example roadmap) while ignoring generic labels such as dashboard/tracer/frontend. This keeps the dashboard hierarchy aligned with the Seeds epic/tracer planning convention without changing dependency semantics.

name: Roadmap detail and filter UI implementation map
description: Roadmap tracer 4 added read-only issue inspection and status filters in web/features/roadmap/RoadmapPanel.tsx. Issue cards are buttons that open an Issue Details dialog showing metadata, labels, timestamps, description, blockers, dependents, and close reason. Filters are local UI state for In Progress, Ready, Blocked, Backlog/Open, and Closed; they do not mutate Seeds or the Roadmap API.

title: Roadmap v1 stays read-only and provider-shaped
rationale: The Roadmap dashboard and /api/roadmap contract use Roadmap DTOs while the current Seeds parser remains isolated in extensions/multi-agent/roadmap.ts. This documents the source boundary for future SQLite/task storage without introducing a storage abstraction or mutation UI in v1.

title: Roadmap defaults to minimal epic-first navigation
rationale: User review found the all-cards Roadmap overloaded the page. The Roadmap UI now starts with compact summary chips, a focus epic callout, collapsible epic rows, subtle filters, and secondary Ready/Blocked queues so users can follow the latest/active epic first and progressively reveal issue details.

name: Agent Type disposable test sessions implementation map
description: Dashboard Agent Type Test/Play flow is implemented in web/features/agent-types/AgentTypesPanel.tsx via AgentTypeTestDialog, wired from web/app.tsx. Backend endpoints in extensions/multi-agent/server.ts create POST /api/agent-types/:name/test-session, POST /api/agent-type-test-sessions/:id/messages, and DELETE /api/agent-type-test-sessions/:id. Sessions are kept out of the live agents map, reuse spawn/capability resolution, reject non-spawnable orchestrator-class definitions, surface runtime tool snapshots/stderr, and clean up by killing the process plus removing the worktree.

Agent Type Test dialog could be dismissed through backdrop/Escape while a disposable test agent was still starting or running, making cleanup ambiguous; Live Agent kill could also appear stale until SSE/refresh updated the list.
resolution: Dialog now supports closeOnBackdrop/closeOnEscape controls; AgentTypeTestDialog disables implicit backdrop/Escape close and requires the explicit Close & Cleanup path. Live Agent kill now optimistically removes the killed card after a successful API response, and the test dialog send button shows a busy Sending state.

description: Disposable Agent Type Test sessions appeared as stale Live Agent cards after receiving a response because spawn stdout handlers broadcast agent-start/agent-delta/agent-end events even when the temporary test agent was intentionally kept out of the live agents map.
resolution: spawnAgent now accepts dashboardVisible:false and stores it on Agent. Its stdout event handling suppresses live dashboard broadcasts for hidden disposable agents. Agent Type Test sessions and Extension Template smoke tests pass dashboardVisible:false while normal orchestrator/dashboard spawns keep the default visible behavior.

description: Agent Type Test sessions could crash the root Pi process with uncaught `write EPIPE` when sending to a disposable agent whose child RPC stdin had already closed.
resolution: Agent command writes now go through `writeAgentCommand`, which preflights process/stdin closure, observes write callbacks, normalizes broken-pipe stream errors into rejected API errors, marks the agent exited, and clears pending turn/RPC promises. Spawn also listens for child stdin errors and rejects pending RPC requests on process close/error.

description: The first Agent Type Test session after root session startup could exit with code 1 because orphan worktree cleanup raced with serialized worktree creation. The child Pi launched against a worktree that cleanup had just deleted, so `/tmp/workspace/.pi/extensions/delegate-agent.ts` was missing.
resolution: Worktree creation now registers a path in an in-memory managedWorktrees set before waiting on the serialized git worktree lock; cleanupOrphanedWorktrees treats managed paths as active and removeWorktree unregisters them. The Agent Type Test dialog also resets the default smoke-test prompt each time it opens.

description: In-memory managed worktree tracking was insufficient for Agent Type Test startup races because orphan cleanup could run from another extension/module instance while a newly-created test worktree was not yet in the agents map, deleting `/tmp/workspace/.pi/extensions/delegate-agent.ts` and causing first-send EPIPE.
resolution: Worktree creation now writes a durable marker under `/tmp/pi-agent-orchestrator-worktree-markers` before git worktree creation completes. Orphan cleanup treats fresh markers as active across module/process instances, and removeWorktree clears the marker.

title: Epic detail modal doubles as task drill-down
rationale: Roadmap users need to inspect an epic's implementation work without leaving the modal. Epic details now use a wider layout that keeps the description prominent while adding a read-only task panel bucketed by actionability: in progress, ready, blocked, backlog, and closed. Clicking a task switches the same modal to that issue detail.

title: Roadmap focus excludes closed epics and avoids inferred open children on closed epics
rationale: A closed epic could become the Focus epic because it was recently updated, and broad shared labels could infer unrelated open tasks under it. Roadmap focus now prefers non-closed epics, and label/title inference no longer attaches open issues to closed epics; closed epics only retain explicitly linked children.

title: Roadmap uses a simplified epic-first layout without global issue filters
rationale: The global status filter bar and separate ready/blocked queue preview duplicated the epic workflow and made the page feel busy. The Roadmap now centers on one Epic Roadmap container: the focus epic opens automatically, other active epics start collapsed, and closed epics are grouped under a separate collapsed section.

title: Roadmap issue drill-down preserves epic return context
rationale: When a user opens a task from an epic detail modal, they are usually comparing sibling tasks within that same epic. The Roadmap now keeps the originating epic id as transient modal navigation state and shows a Back to epic button on the task detail view, while normal roadmap issue selection still opens details without back context.

title: Roadmap epic modal back navigation persists through related issue links
rationale: Users drilling into an epic may follow task blockers or dependents before returning to the epic's task list. The modal now keeps the originating epic context across task, blocker, and dependent navigation, and renders a compact arrow-only back affordance instead of a verbose labeled button.

title: Roadmap epic return context includes epic dependency links
rationale: Dependency links in an epic detail modal are still part of the user's epic drill-down flow, not standalone issue navigation. The modal now derives return context from either the existing back issue or the currently open epic, so blockers/dependents clicked directly from epic details also show the compact back arrow.

title: Docker/process mode replaces bwrap-first spawning for the prototype
rationale: The orchestrator should run Pi inside a Docker/devcontainer environment and spawn child Pi RPC agents as normal processes in git worktrees. Docker is the outer sandbox and cross-machine/Windows-friendly reproducible environment, so preserving bwrap fallback or adding nested per-agent containers is unnecessary prototype complexity. This also simplifies Agent Type tests, extension smoke tests, and future protocol gates, while shifting role safety to exact tool allowlists and agent-type/template capability resolution.

title: Orchestrator Libraries use container paths as runtime truth
rationale: When Pi runs inside Docker, external user/team Orchestrator Libraries must be bind-mounted before Pi starts and registered by their stable container paths, commonly under /orchestrator-libraries/<name>. The dashboard can auto-discover in-repo libraries and mounted external libraries, but it cannot access arbitrary host paths from inside the container. Host paths belong in devcontainer/Docker launch config; runtime discovery, precedence, and extension loading should use container-visible paths.

title: Network hardening is deferred behind Docker/process migration
rationale: Containerized orchestration should first land Docker/devcontainer, direct process launch, capability allowlists, mounted library discovery, and handoff artifacts. Web/network guardrails are captured as a P4 follow-up: likely a companion Pi guardrail extension around pi-web-access plus container/proxy network enforcement so audited web tools cannot be bypassed by raw bash egress.

title: Orchestrator Library compatibility ranges gate library validity
rationale: Orchestrator Library manifests now validate `compatibility.piAgentOrchestrator` against the package version using a small supported semver subset. Incompatible future requirements and malformed compatibility values are errors, so invalid libraries remain discoverable with diagnostics but their resources are not loaded. Bootstrap scaffolds compatibility from the current package version to avoid creating self-incompatible libraries.

title: Orchestrator Libraries are auto-discovered from bounded app paths
rationale: User clarified that Orchestrator Libraries should not be manually registered by arbitrary dashboard paths. Repo-local libraries belong under the app-owned `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/libraries/<name>/` folder, external libraries must be bind-mounted into `/orchestrator-libraries/<name>/`, and discovery scans those manifest locations deterministically. The dashboard should offer enable/disable, not load-order or arbitrary path editing; conflicting enabled libraries should surface diagnostics until one is disabled. Mounted external libraries may be writable so agents can manage their resources.

title: External Orchestrator Libraries mount under the app-owned project directory
rationale: External Orchestrator Library repos should still be bind-mounted, but the container target should be inside `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/external-libraries/<name>/` rather than a root-level `/orchestrator-libraries` directory. This keeps all project-local Pi Orchestrator state under one app-owned `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/` namespace while distinguishing repo-local libraries from mounted external-library repos.

title: Spawned agent tool allowlists are exact, including extension tools
rationale: Capability lockdown for scouts/builders requires `--tools` to remain active even when extra extensions are loaded, and the runtime must not implicitly append `delegate`; agent definitions/templates must list every built-in, extension, or custom tool they need.

title: Dashboard dynamic refresh should use push invalidations and pull data
rationale: The dashboard already has an SSE channel for live agent events. For app-wide dynamic behavior, the server should broadcast lightweight resource-changed invalidations while feature panels refetch their normal HTTP DTOs when active or on tab activation. This keeps SSE payloads small, preserves provider-shaped APIs like /api/roadmap, and lets runtime state overlay persisted Seeds/Mulch/resource data without forcing every live detail into durable stores.

title: Dashboard modals default to explicit close only
rationale: Backdrop clicks and Escape can accidentally dismiss modals while selecting/copying text or editing form data. The shared Dialog now defaults backdrop/Escape dismissal off, and data-entry modals opt into dirty close confirmation for X/Cancel so unsaved edits are not discarded silently.

title: Spawned-agent observability must be first-class before relying on delegated workflows
rationale: The lead-15e6 handoff test showed that a prompt can be written to a spawned RPC agent while the dashboard still appears idle and silent. Trustworthy orchestration requires per-agent lifecycle/transcript visibility: launch metadata, prompts/steers, turn/preflight state, assistant/tool events, stderr/errors, exits/kills, and on-demand inspection without loading all child context into the root orchestrator.

name: Containerized runtime baseline implementation map
description: Docker/devcontainer baseline lives in Dockerfile and .devcontainer/devcontainer.json. docs/container-runtime.md documents running Pi inside the container, named volumes for /home/node/.pi and Bun cache, dashboard port 18765, and bind-mounting external Orchestrator Libraries under .pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/external-libraries/<name>. README links to the container runtime docs and .gitignore excludes external-libraries mounts.

name: Direct process spawned-agent launch implementation map
description: Direct child-process launch now lives in extensions/multi-agent/spawn.ts via buildProcessLaunch(), which launches Pi RPC with cwd set to the agent worktree and passes worktree-local prompt/extension paths directly. extensions/delegate-agent.ts resolves comms under process.cwd()/.pi/comms. extensions/multi-agent/ext-discovery.ts returns copied extension paths as real worktree paths. Tests in tests/spawn-worktree.test.ts cover no /tmp/workspace path translation and worktree cwd launch metadata.

title: Devcontainer installs the live checkout as the orchestrator package
rationale: For pi-agent-orchestrator self-development, the devcontainer should run bun install/build and then pi install /workspaces/pi-agent-orchestrator so Pi loads extensions/skills from the mounted repository rather than a copied npm package. Bundled Pi helper packages are exposed through the package manifest, and project settings no longer need to list those helper packages separately.

title: Dockerfile tracks latest Bun and Pi CLI
rationale: For the development container baseline, the Dockerfile installs Bun via the default installer and installs @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent without a pinned version so new devcontainers pick up the latest runtime tooling. Package-level dev dependencies remain responsible for repository validation/build consistency.

name: Live Agent card preview vs Inspect responsibilities
description: For spawned-agent observability, keep the Live Agent card as the operator-friendly preview: show local pending sends immediately, render assistant text as Markdown, and surface concise runtime metadata such as model/turns/context. Keep Inspect as the raw timeline/debug view with plain event text so users can diagnose exact agent events without losing readable card UX.

name: Agent observability timeline implementation map
description: Agent timeline DTO construction lives in extensions/multi-agent/timeline.ts. /api/agents/:name/events in extensions/multi-agent/server.ts now returns timeline plus backwards-compatible fields, archives killed/emergency-stopped agent timelines in memory, and web/app.tsx renders sectioned InspectTimeline UI. Stderr snippets are recorded in extensions/multi-agent/spawn.ts.

name: Spawned-agent debug artifact implementation map
description: Tracer 4 persists per-agent operational timelines under `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/sessions/<session>/agents/<agent>/timeline.json` via `extensions/multi-agent/debug-artifacts.ts`. Spawn/send/steer/stderr/exit/kill paths call persistence from `spawn.ts`, `send.ts`, and `server.ts`; `/api/agents/:name/events` falls back to persisted timelines for recently exited agents. Artifacts are redacted/size-limited operational diagnostics, not Seeds or Mulch state.

Live Agent cards could disappear for about two seconds every ~20 seconds even while agents were still alive because the dashboard SSE /events stream stayed silent when agents were idle, letting browser/proxy idle timeouts close the EventSource and force reconnect/init cycles.
resolution: The SSE endpoint now flushes headers and emits `: heartbeat` comment frames every 10 seconds with `Cache-Control: no-cache, no-transform`; tests cover heartbeat delivery with an accelerated interval.

name: Seeds dependency guard implementation map
description: The multi-agent extension enforces Seeds dependency semantics in extensions/multi-agent/seeds-guard.ts and wires it in extensions/multi-agent/index.ts via before_agent_start prompt injection and tool_call blocking. Tests live in tests/seeds-guard.test.ts.

title: Pi Lattice rename preserves orchestrator as role/protocol language
rationale: Product and resource branding should use Pi Lattice and Lattice Library. User-facing root session configuration is Root Profile, while root orchestrator remains valid descriptive role language and Orchestration Protocol remains valid protocol terminology. Legacy on-disk/API compatibility names such as orchestratorProfiles, orchestrator-profiles, orchestrator-library.json, pi-orchestrator-library/v1, and piAgentOrchestrator remain accepted while compatibility work is active.

title: Pi Lattice rename drops legacy compatibility aliases
rationale: Current rename policy requires new Pi Lattice/Lattice Library names only. Legacy manifest names, schemas, settings namespaces, endpoint aliases, constants, and auto-discovery paths should be removed rather than preserved behind compatibility branches.

name: Roadmap mutation API implementation map
description: Tracer 1 added validated Roadmap issue mutations in extensions/multi-agent/roadmap.ts via validateRoadmapIssuePatch/updateRoadmapIssue and PATCH /api/roadmap/issues/:id in extensions/multi-agent/server.ts. Mutations call Seeds through an injectable runSeedsCommand, keeping UI/API provider-shaped and avoiding direct .seeds/issues.jsonl edits.

name: Roadmap status controls implementation map
description: Tracer 2 added Roadmap status controls in web/features/roadmap/RoadmapPanel.tsx. IssueDetailDialog owns the status select/save/error UI, calls PATCH /api/roadmap/issues/:id through the provider-shaped Roadmap API, and updates local RoadmapOverview from the response while preserving selected issue/epic drill-down context. UI coverage lives in tests/roadmap-panel.test.tsx with happy-dom render helpers.

name: Roadmap description editing implementation map
description: Tracer 3 added inline Roadmap description editing in web/features/roadmap/RoadmapPanel.tsx. IssueDetailDialog owns edit/save/cancel textarea state, sends PATCH /api/roadmap/issues/:id with {description}, keeps failed edits in-place with inline/logged errors, and uses Dialog confirmOnClose when the draft is dirty. UI coverage is in tests/roadmap-panel.test.tsx.

name: Roadmap start-work action implementation map
description: Tracer 4 added Roadmap Start work actions in web/features/roadmap/RoadmapPanel.tsx, reusing the existing Roadmap mutation API with PATCH { status: "in_progress" } and leaving assignee semantics out of scope because the dashboard has no current-user source.

name: Roadmap copy issue ID implementation map
description: Tracer 5 added CopyIssueIdButton in web/features/roadmap/RoadmapPanel.tsx and wires it into epic rows, issue cards, issue detail headers, and dependency rows. Clickable card/row wrappers use role=button divs so nested copy buttons can stop propagation without invalid button nesting.

name: Live Agents add flow uses an in-grid draft card
description: The Live Agents panel now treats spawning as a draft/ghost agent card rendered first in the same responsive grid as active AgentCard entries. The draft card starts collapsed with an Add Agent affordance, expands in place for spawn details, cancels/resets locally, and collapses after successful spawn so adding agents feels like creating another card in the collection rather than using a separate wide form.
Live Agent cards could remain stuck on a pending/waiting preview or hydrate blank after refresh even though Inspect had the assistant response.
resolution: Serialize latest assistant preview text in `/api/agents`, centralize dashboard agent-state merging to clear stale pendingSend/turnDiagnostics when terminal/idle updates arrive, and keep preview/loading card dimensions fixed to avoid grid jumps.

title: Spawned child agents use explicit Pi session IDs
rationale: Pi 0.76 added `--session-id`; Pi Lattice should launch spawned RPC agents with deterministic `pi-lattice.<run>.<agent>` session IDs instead of `--no-session` so each child agent has a durable native Pi transcript for debugging and later E2E assertions while avoiding collisions across orchestrator runs.

title: Native Pi session metadata is part of spawned-agent observability
rationale: Pi Lattice stores child RPC `get_state` session metadata on `Agent.nativeSession` and carries it through dashboard/API/timeline/debug artifacts, while launch-time deterministic `--session-id` remains an immediate fallback until RPC state is available.

title: Internal child RPC bash commands default to context exclusion
rationale: Pi 0.76 added RPC bash `excludeFromContext`; Pi Lattice exposes `rpcBashCommand` so future orchestrator maintenance/preflight bash calls against child agents opt out of the child model context by default, preserving the option to override explicitly.

name: Pi retry provider settings
description: Pi project settings support retry.provider.timeoutMs, retry.provider.maxRetries, and retry.provider.maxRetryDelayMs. This project sets provider maxRetries to 0 and caps provider retry delay to avoid SDK/provider quota waits before Pi can surface errors.

title: Pi Lattice workflows are Lattice Library YAML resources with generated Mermaid previews
rationale: Workflow orchestration should be user-owned and version-controlled under Lattice Libraries. YAML is the source of truth for readability/diffability; Mermaid is generated only for visual confirmation. The dashboard editor should remain lightweight with structured text/form controls and live preview rather than a heavy canvas dependency.

title: AI-assisted workflow authoring uses validated full YAML replacement with extension-owned repair loops
rationale: Models can propose full Workflow YAML replacements, but the dashboard/extension owns validation, automatic repair feedback, retry limits, diff/preview, and final persistence. This keeps authoring ergonomic while preventing invalid workflows or direct unapproved file writes. V1 uses a workflow-authoring SKILL.md and existing Pi model discovery for assistant model selection.

title: Workflow runtime v1 guides the root orchestrator and preserves root-owned spawning
rationale: For the first runtime iteration, selected workflows guide the root orchestrator rather than directly auto-driving every spawn/action. Lead and specialist agents do not spawn directly; they request transitions or handoffs while the root remains durable owner of agent creation and metadata writes. Streaming-aware @agent syntax will route steers/follow-ups into active workflow runs.

title: WorkflowRun owns runtime state while v1 agent communication stays parent-child only
rationale: Workflow runtime should keep live topology, audit events, handoffs, and guardrail enforcement in an extension-owned WorkflowRun control plane rather than flooding root model context. Agents may spawn/message only direct children through workflow-authorized tools, and children report summaries/handoffs back to their parent; arbitrary peer routing and grandchild-to-grandparent communication are deferred to avoid schema/UI complexity.

title: Workflow v1 uses YAML-only resources and immediate parent-child runtime semantics
rationale: Workflow authoring should persist YAML only, with JSON limited to internal DTO/API shapes. Static workflow Mermaid previews show optional child-spawn capabilities as generated blueprint diagrams, while Live Agents/runtime overlays show actual WorkflowRun topology. Runtime parent means the live agent instance that spawned a child through WorkflowRun tools; communication is only immediate parent-child, while multi-depth spawning may exist without skipped-level routing. Edges remain control-flow only; spawn policy and handoff requirements are separate schema concepts.

title: Roadmap epic grouping uses explicit membership only
rationale: Shared-label/title inference caused new epics with broad labels to show unrelated children and blocker counts. Roadmap hierarchy now derives epic children from explicit `Part of <epic-id>` issue descriptions, while dependency edges remain hard blockers and are displayed as dependency metadata rather than membership.

title: Roadmap Epic Board v1 is visualization-only with exclusive column precedence
rationale: Expanded epic Kanban semantics now treat explicit `Part of <epic-id>` as membership and hard Seeds dependencies only as blocker metadata. Board cards render in one column by precedence: Done, Blocked, Current Focus from Seeds extensions, In Progress, Ready, then Backlog/Open. V1 explicitly excludes Review/Validate columns, Roadmap Start Work, drag/drop mutation, and agent spawn/handoff so workflow/orchestrator tools own work execution.
